name: projection-patterns
description: Build read models and projections from event streams. Use when implementing CQRS read sides, building materialized views, or optimizing query performance in event-sourced systems.
capabilities: Explain CQRS projection architecture and component roles
Provide Python code templates for building projectors
Guide implementation of checkpoint-based event processing
Show patterns for Elasticsearch and SQL read models
Demonstrate aggregation and multi-table projection techniques
Validate projection idempotency and error handling
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill contains documentation and Python code examples for CQRS projection patterns. Static analyzer flagged markdown code blocks as shell commands (false positive). URLs are documentation references, not network calls. No executable code, cryptographic operations, or system access detected. Safe for publication.
valueStatement: Struggling with query performance in event-sourced systems? This skill provides proven patterns for building efficient read models and projections from event streams.
userTitle: Build CQRS Projections and Read Models
limitations: Provides guidance only - does not execute code or connect to databases
Code examples are Python-focused - other languages require adaptation
Assumes familiarity with event sourcing fundamentals
Does not provide production monitoring or observability setup

useCases.targetUser: Backend engineers implementing order management systems
useCases.title: E-commerce Order Projections
useCases.description: Build read models that aggregate order events into customer-facing summaries with real-time status updates.

useCases.targetUser: Developers building search functionality in event-sourced applications
useCases.title: Search Index from Events
useCases.description: Create Elasticsearch projections that keep product catalogs in sync with domain events for full-text search.

useCases.targetUser: Data engineers building real-time analytics dashboards
useCases.title: Analytics Dashboard Data
useCases.description: Implement aggregating projections that compute daily metrics like sales totals and user activity.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Projection Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to build a read model for order events. Show me how to create a basic projector that handles OrderCreated and OrderUpdated events, stores checkpoints, and updates a SQL table.

promptTemplates.title: Elasticsearch Integration
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a projection that syncs product domain events to Elasticsearch. Include handling for ProductCreated, ProductUpdated, and ProductDeleted events with proper error handling.

promptTemplates.title: Aggregation Pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to build an aggregating projection that computes daily sales metrics from OrderCompleted events. Include UPSERT logic for efficient updates.

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Table Transactional Projection
promptTemplates.prompt: I need a projection that updates multiple tables atomically when processing CustomerOrderPlaced events. Show transaction handling and rollback patterns.
promptTemplates.scenario: Complex domain with related read models

bestPractices: Make projections idempotent - safe to replay events multiple times without side effects
Store checkpoints after each event to enable resume after failures
Use database transactions for multi-table updates to maintain consistency
antiPatterns: Coupling projections together - each projection should be independent and isolatable
Skipping error handling - unhandled exceptions stop projection and cause data lag
Processing events out of order - always respect global_position ordering

faq.question: What is the difference between a projection and a read model?
faq.answer: A projection is the process that transforms events into a read model. The read model is the resulting data structure (database tables, cache, search index) optimized for queries.

faq.question: How do I handle events that arrive out of order?
faq.answer: Use global_position from the event store to ensure strict ordering. Reject or buffer events that arrive before their predecessors. The event store guarantees order, not the message bus.

faq.question: Can I rebuild a projection if the read model gets corrupted?
faq.answer: Yes. Delete the projection checkpoint and clear the read model tables, then re-run from position 0. This requires projections to be idempotent.

faq.question: Should projections be synchronous or asynchronous?
faq.answer: Use asynchronous processing for scalability. Projections typically run as background workers consuming from the event stream, not in the write transaction.

faq.question: How do I handle projection failures?
faq.answer: Log errors with full event context, alert on repeated failures, and implement retry logic with exponential backoff. Consider a dead-letter queue for poison events.

faq.question: What database should I use for read models?
faq.answer: Choose based on query patterns: PostgreSQL for relational queries, Elasticsearch for full-text search, Redis for caching, or column stores for analytics. Multiple projections can use different databases.
